title: Logbook Roadmap Schema
description: Product-level roadmap with rolling context. Orchestrates phases and milestones that spawn individual logbooks. Sits above logbook_software_schema and logbook_general_schema in the artifact hierarchy: blueprint (WHAT/WHY) → roadmap (WHEN/ORDER) → logbook (HOW).
$comment: One per product. This is the strategic orchestrator — it tracks phases, milestones, and decisions at the product level. Detailed implementation lives in individual logbooks referenced via milestones[].logbook_ref. Do NOT duplicate logbook-level detail (completion_guides, scope.files, recommendations) here.

product.$comment: Set at creation. Update last_updated on every write operation to this file. Status reflects the overall product lifecycle, not any individual phase.

name.$comment: The public-facing name. Use this consistently across all references.

codename.description: Internal codename if different from public name.
codename.$comment: Optional. Useful when the product has an internal working name different from the marketed name.

description.description: One-paragraph product description at business level.
description.$comment: What it does, for whom, why it matters. No technical jargon. A stakeholder should understand this without context.

owner.description: Product owner — person ultimately responsible for product decisions.
owner.$comment: Name or identifier. This person approves phase transitions and major decisions.

created_at.description: UTC timestamp when this roadmap was created. Immutable.
created_at.$comment: Set once at creation. Never modify.

last_updated.description: UTC timestamp of last modification to this file.
last_updated.$comment: Update on every write operation. Reflects freshness of the roadmap.

status.$comment: planning: still designing phases/vision. active: at least one phase in progress. paused: development on hold. completed: all phases done. archived: no longer maintained.

vision.description: Product vision — the strategic WHY. Concise summary that guides prioritization without opening the full blueprint.
vision.$comment: If a product_blueprint exists, extract and compress from there. If not, capture directly from product owner. Keep each field concise — the blueprint has the full detail.

problem.$comment: One paragraph. Answer: What hurts? Why is it real? Why is it unsolved?

mission.description: What the product does day-to-day for users.
mission.$comment: Operational, not aspirational. The daily commitment to users. Aligns with blueprint section 4.

target_users.description: User types that interact with this product.
target_users.$comment: One string per user type. Format: 'Role — what they need from the product'. Keep to 2-5 users. These map to roles and permissions in the system.

success_criteria.description: How we know the product is working.
success_criteria.$comment: Optional. Verifiable statements. Aligns with blueprint section 13 (metrics). If the blueprint exists, extract the top 3-5 most important metrics.

phases.description: Ordered development phases. Each groups related milestones into a deliverable unit.
phases.$comment: Phases execute sequentially unless depends_on says otherwise. Only ONE phase should be 'active' at a time. When all milestones in a phase are 'completed', mark the phase as 'completed' and activate the next eligible phase.

decisions.description: Product-level decisions log. Once recorded, a decision stands unless explicitly revised with a new entry.
decisions.$comment: Add entry for every significant decision: architecture choice, scope change, technology selection, feature cut, role definition. If a decision isn't here, it wasn't decided. This prevents re-discussion. Aligns with blueprint section 15.

open_questions.description: Questions that cannot be answered yet. When resolved, move answer to decisions if it was a decision, or remove if it was informational.
open_questions.$comment: Add questions discovered during planning, client meetings, or implementation. Review at every session start. Aligns with blueprint section 16.

recent_context.description: Running log of recent interactions, findings, decisions, and progress at the product level. Newest entries at index 0.
recent_context.$comment: Same mechanics as logbook_software_schema. Prepend new entries at index 0. When array exceeds maxItems, remove last (oldest) entry and summarize to history_summary. Use for product-level context only — ticket-level progress goes in individual logbooks.

history_summary.description: Compact summaries of entries removed from recent_context. Preserves long-term continuity.
history_summary.$comment: Same mechanics as logbook_software_schema. Write here ONLY when truncating recent_context. Max 140 chars per entry. Prepend at index 0.

future_reminders.$comment: Same mechanics as logbook_software_schema. Only add on explicit user request. Check at session start and surface any due reminders.

phase.description: A development phase grouping related milestones into a deliverable unit.
phase.$comment: Phases are the highest organizational unit. Each phase should represent a cohesive set of capabilities that can be released or demonstrated together.

id.description: Unique phase identifier. Immutable once created.
id.$comment: Start at 1, increment by 1. Never reuse IDs even if a phase is removed.

name.$comment: Descriptive and concise. E.g., 'Foundation', 'Core Features', 'Sync Protocol'. Used as reference in context entries and conversations.

description.description: What this phase delivers and why it comes at this point in the sequence.
description.$comment: Include the business value and the technical rationale for ordering. A stakeholder should understand why this phase matters.

status.$comment: not_started: waiting for dependencies. active: at least one milestone in progress (only ONE phase active at a time). completed: all milestones done. blocked: waiting on external dependency outside project control.

depends_on.description: IDs of phases that must be 'completed' before this phase can start.
depends_on.$comment: Empty array means no dependencies — phase can start when previous phases by order allow. Use this for non-sequential dependencies (e.g., phase 4 depends on phase 1 but not 2 or 3).

milestones.$comment: Milestones within a phase can be worked in parallel. Each milestone can spawn one or more logbooks via logbook_ref. When all milestones are 'completed', mark the phase as 'completed'.

milestone.description: A verifiable milestone within a phase. Can spawn individual logbooks for detailed execution.
milestone.$comment: Milestones are the bridge between roadmap (strategic) and logbooks (tactical). Keep content as a verifiable outcome statement. When a logbook is created for this milestone, set logbook_ref to the filename.

id.$comment: Format: 'N.M' where N is the phase ID and M is sequential within the phase. E.g., '1.1', '1.2', '2.1'. Never reuse.

content.description: Verifiable outcome statement. When this is true, the milestone is done.
content.$comment: Write as outcome, not task. Validate: Can you answer 'Is this done?' with yes/no? If not, rewrite. This becomes the seed for the spawned logbook's ticket.description.

status.$comment: Track independently from phase status. A milestone is 'completed' when its outcome statement is verified true — either by code inspection (if logbook exists) or by owner confirmation.

logbook_ref.description: Filename of the spawned logbook that implements this milestone.
logbook_ref.$comment: Null until a logbook is created via logbook-create command. Set to filename (e.g., 'logbook_auth_system.json') when the logbook is created. One milestone can reference one logbook. If a milestone needs multiple logbooks, split into multiple milestones.

decision.description: A product-level decision with context. Once here, it stands unless explicitly revised.
decision.$comment: Record every significant decision: architecture choices, scope changes, technology selections, feature cuts, role definitions. Include enough context that a future reader understands WHY without asking.

id.$comment: Start at 1, increment by 1. Never reuse.

decision.$comment: Clear, unambiguous statement. E.g., 'Use Firebase Auth with custom claims for role management' not 'We might use Firebase'.

context.description: Why this was decided and what alternatives were considered.
context.$comment: Include: the trigger (what forced the decision), alternatives evaluated, and why this option won. Prevents future re-discussion.

impact.description: How much this decision affects the product direction.
impact.$comment: high: changes architecture, scope, or user experience fundamentally. medium: affects a phase or feature significantly. low: implementation detail with limited blast radius.

open_question.description: A question that cannot be answered yet.
open_question.$comment: When resolved: set status to 'resolved', fill resolution, and if the answer was a decision, also add a decision entry. Do not delete — keep for audit trail.

id.$comment: Start at 1, increment by 1. Never reuse.

question.description: The question we cannot answer yet.
question.$comment: Be specific. Bad: 'What about auth?' Good: 'Should client role have read access to logbook recommendations field?'

status.description: Whether the question has been answered.
status.$comment: Set to 'resolved' only when a definitive answer exists. If the answer is a decision, also create a decision entry.

resolution.description: How the question was resolved. Null while open.
resolution.$comment: Fill when status changes to 'resolved'. Include who decided and why.

context_entry.description: Single context entry representing a product-level interaction, finding, or decision.
context_entry.$comment: Same structure as logbook_software_schema.recent_context items. Use for product-level context only — ticket-level progress belongs in individual logbooks.

id.$comment: Start at 1, increment by 1 globally across all entries. Never reuse IDs.

created_at.description: UTC timestamp when entry was created. Immutable.
created_at.$comment: Set once at creation. Never modify.

content.description: Entry content describing product-level action, decision, finding, or progress.
content.$comment: Write concisely but completely. Include: what happened, why it matters, any decisions made. Future agents use this to understand product context.

history_entry.description: Compressed summary of a removed recent_context entry.
history_entry.$comment: Same structure as logbook_software_schema.history_summary items. Max 140 chars. Capture essential information only.

id.$comment: Separate sequence from recent_context IDs. Start at 1, increment by 1.

content.$comment: Tweet-length. Focus on: what was done, outcome, or key decision.

reminder.description: A scheduled reminder for a future session.
reminder.$comment: Same structure as logbook_software_schema.future_reminders items. Only add on explicit user request. Surface when current time >= when.

id.$comment: Start at 1, increment by 1. Never reuse.

content.$comment: Write exactly what user wants to be reminded about. Include context needed.

when.$comment: Compare against current time at session start. Surface if current time >= when.
